In the monumental list of consent criteria by parents on a pupil's page, the ones where consent has NOT been given should go automatically to the top or be made VERY visible. Very easy to miss, particularly if parent has given consent to 20 different things but not 1 and then NEVER mentioned it, and the lack of consent is at the very bottom of the long list mixed in with all the consent that has been given. Human error is amplified in these cases. Extremely important information on a pupil's page should stand out more/be made more obvious. This is a real downside to Arbor. The behaviour system for logging is also incredibly confusing and because it reports to the Trust from a certain level, there is a lot of hesitation about how to classify something. Granted, this may just take time getting used to. Arbor makes doing the register 10x easier - excellent. Quick and easy access. Logging behaviour is easy enough but some of the drop down options are confusing. Analysing data? Completely confusing on how to get there and how to access different checkpoints, different mark ups, analysis. Not to mention inputting analysis. For how it's improved my work? Well, headship have had to run several INSET meetings to go through how to do it. So not necessarily saving us time. I think there is a lot of good in the system, but it needs to be simplified (some of the language maybe even changed). The tutorials available are helpful to a degree but there is so much that we are not using in the system because we don't know it exists/don't know how to/find it confusing. And we forget how to get somewhere after watching a tutorial because there are so many steps to get there. Again, a lot of promise but teachers are incredibly busy throughout the day. Some simplifications should be made for better quick /daily use - particularly for the essentials for data, behaviour logging, and key information about a child.

Arbor is a strong platform for operational tasks such as registers, behaviour logging and attendance, seating plan, tracking. These areas are intuitive and work reliably in day to day school use. However, data entry and reporting workflows represent a significant weakness. Accessing pupil data input and reporting sections typically requires multiple layers of navigation, often seven to eight clicks before reaching the required area. This makes routine data tasks unnecessarily time consuming and inefficient. By comparison, legacy systems such as SIMS and platforms like Go 4 Schools offer more streamlined and intuitive data entry processes. Arbor’s current design places a high cognitive and time burden on users for tasks that are completed frequently and under time pressure. This area requires urgent review. Improvements to navigation depth, shortcut access and simplified data input workflows would significantly enhance usability and staff adoption. Addressing this would materially improve Arbor’s overall effectiveness, particularly for data heavy roles such as middle and senior leaders. Overall, Arbor is a well designed system with clear strengths. Resolving this specific issue would elevate it from a strong platform to an excellent one.

Arbor is a great platform for teachers, but it lacks some essential features that would genuinely help busy staff — I’m actually a little surprised these haven’t been implemented yet. The register page doesn’t allow copying student names, which makes simple tasks unnecessarily time-consuming. There are no totals displayed (e.g., the number of students in the list), which would be helpful at a glance. There’s no warning when saving a register with missing marks, increasing the risk of accidental incomplete submissions. The system doesn’t flag inconsistencies, such as marking a student present when they were absent in the previous lesson. Additional features are needed, such as an option to automatically generate a random seating plan when required. These are just a few examples, but there are many more areas where small improvements could make a big difference.

Comments: This system is not suitable for use with secondary schools. It has no concept of taking multiple registers in a school day with different classes. The potential action is hidden behind so many sub-menus, that staff are just giving up on the tool. Setting of detentions is arduous and many will bypass the system & send an e-mail directly just to expedite the process. Detentions do not send e-mails to secondary school pupils - why not?! The annoying pop-ups and tool-tips hover over the next cell that you may need to enter data into. You cannot view your form's attendance for the day/week/month, but only for their form-PSHE periods when you already know if they're present as they are in front of you. Getting data out of the system pertaining to prior examinations is randomly chaotic. You cannot find previous detentions you have entered/logged. Minimal training has not helped the cause. SIMS.Net may not have web access, but for secondary schools, it meant a rapid response for the daily functions that we have to do. Arbor assumes a huge screen is available to the end user for the infinite scrolling in two frames, so you have to consider which one you are scrolling in. Entering report/grade-card data is not pleasant. Using the keyboard is actively discouraged, so it's constant use via the mouse, which does not help all. It's not quite clear as to what is a link, a highlighted drop down, a roll-over colour change, or a field to enter. It's not something I would recommend to a secondary school teacher if they have a varied teaching timetable.

Navigation & Workflows: The route to the calendar is overly click-heavy and slows staff down. Teaching timetables and current lessons should be the default landing page, as this is the core function staff need to access immediately. Student Photos & Visibility: The current student profile images are too small and low in resolution to be practically useful. Increasing their scale and clarity would improve identification and reduce mistakes. Information Placement: Key student information could be layered or displayed consistently to one side of the headshot, so staff can quickly understand essential details without having to open multiple panels. Additional areas that could be improved: • Loading speeds: Some pages refresh slowly, especially during busy periods, which interrupts the flow of teaching and behaviour logging. • Behaviour logging workflow: Too many sub-menus and not enough shortcuts for common entries. Ideally, staff should be able to log a behaviour in under 10 seconds, mid-lesson, without an archaeological dig. • Consistency of layout: Different pages use different layouts and button placements, which increases cognitive load - especially when switching rapidly between tasks in a lesson. • Mobile view usability: On phones, the spacing and scaling often force unnecessary scroll-hunting. A simplified, teaching-focused mobile layout would help enormously. • Class lists: Class lists could benefit from some quicker sorting options? is that what its called? (e.g., alphabetically, SEN flags, PP, attendance) without having to open separate filters.

There is a lot which is good about Arbor - we have recently made the swap from another system so there have been some teething issues, but that is not necessarily something that feels appropriate to feed back here. I have tried to list below the things which are outside the scope of school-based teething problems, and stick to features that would be great to add or adjust. 1. There are some bugs which are frustrating, eg. when completing the register, sometimes in the 'take register' section I can see that students are marked absent already, but in the 'my classroom' view, they don't have anything marked at all. This makes it easy to override previous entries, losing comments. 2. When setting homework, we always want to set as 'submit via arbor', or the students don't have a way of marking a task as completed, making it very hard to manage what they have outstanding. I'd really like for this to be set by default (or at least for there to be a school level setting which means schools can set it by default if appropriate). Similarly, I'd really like for the 'no mark' option to be set by default, or again for the school to be able to set this. 3. When completing data sheets (assessments), the class list is sometimes incorrect because the date doesn't default to the current date. At the start of the academic year, there are often changes which mean that people move classes. Even if the register is then up to date, the assessments list then isn't until it is changed. It would be helpful for it to default to the current date, and therefore use the most up to date class list by default. 4. In my calendar at the side of the screen, the lessons are given by their times. It would be much quicker to process if these could be given different labels, rather than just being the time, eg. Form, Period 1, Period 2 etc. 5. When logging an incident (which is the way my school logs negative behaviour points), at the moment I am having to look up the students' form tutor, and assign the incident to them manually. It would be really helpful if there was the option (a school-level setting again perhaps) for the the incident to be automatically assigned to the form tutor(s), rather than to the member of staff actually logging the incident. 6. When completing a register, I really like that it automatically scrolls the page down slightly, to move to the next student. However, this only happens when marking a student present. It would be good if this also happened when marking a student absent or late. Thank you for reading this feedback, and I look forward to seeing any changes that are made as they are rolled out :)
